items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.correct: Features of the tax and benefit system that automatically cushion the economy without new legislation — e.g. unemployment benefits rise in slumps; tax revenues fall as incomes fall, supporting demand.
items.misconception: Automatic stabilisers are interest rate changes by the central bank.
items.random_wrong: Automatic stabilisers are special government emergency funds.
items.why: Stabilisers are fiscal (tax/benefit) and automatic (no decision needed) — distinct from monetary policy or discretionary stimulus. Students confuse them with central bank action or with one-off bailout funds.

items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.q: A rise in consumer income shifts demand for a normal good:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Right. “Left” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Above equilibrium price, the market shows:
items.why: Correct answer: Surplus. “Shortage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
